specialties.name: Aircraft Electrical/Instrument/Flight Control Systems Technician, Fixed Wing, IMA
specialties.what_it_is: You work in an Intermediate Maintenance Activity (IMA) — a back-shop, not the flight line. Aircraft come out of the squadron and broken electrical, instrument and flight-control components come to you. You test them on the bench, isolate the fault down to the module, card or printed circuit board, repair or replace it, and certify the part back into the supply system. Expect bench work, test sets, wiring diagrams and soldering rather than turning wrenches on an aircraft.

Marine Corps COOL maps this MOS to electronics credentials (NCATT/ASTM Aircraft Electronics Technician, ETA and SpaceTEC electronics certs) — it does NOT map it to the FAA Airframe & Powerplant (A&P) mechanic certificate, which is the license most civilian aircraft-maintenance jobs are built around. To close the gap: FAA rules let you count documented military maintenance experience toward A&P eligibility, but you must still be signed off and pass the FAA written, oral and practical tests yourself. Separately, civilian avionics work at an FAA repair station is normally done under a certificated repairman or A&P holder, so plan on either the A&P route or an employer-sponsored repairman certificate. Get the NCATT AET and the ETA electronics certs while you are still in — COOL funds them.

specialties.name: Advanced Automatic Test Equipment Technician, IMA
specialties.what_it_is: You maintain and repair the Reconfigurable Transportable Consolidated Automated Support System (RTCASS) — the big automated test stations the Marine Corps uses to diagnose aircraft electronic boxes. When a fault cannot be isolated by the normal procedures, the part comes to your station and you go deeper. This is troubleshooting the test equipment itself as much as the aircraft parts, so it is heavy on electronics theory, test benches and diagnostic software.

specialties.what_it_is: You are the person who makes sure the test equipment itself is telling the truth. civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: The skills transfer to civilian HVAC/refrigeration work, but the license does not. Civilian HVAC is a licensed trade in most states and — critically — anyone who buys, handles or disposes of refrigerant must hold an EPA Section 608 Technician Certification. Nothing in the MOS pipeline awards it. Marine Corps COOL maps this MOS to Air Conditioning Installation and Air Conditioning Service credentials plus the electronics certs; those are exams you take, not things you are handed. To close the gap: get EPA 608 (cheap, and the single most important card in the trade), then a state HVAC license or an apprenticeship, and use COOL funding for the AC certs while you still have it.

specialties.name: Aircraft Ordnance Technician
specialties.what_it_is: You build up, load and download the weapons a Marine aircraft carries — bombs, missiles, rockets, gun systems — and you maintain the armament equipment on the aircraft itself. Work happens on the flight line in aircrews and load teams, frequently at night, in poor weather and against a launch clock. Because you handle live explosives, the medical, certification and licensing rules around you are unusually strict.

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS national, May 2024, SOC 49-9071 (Maintenance and Repair Workers, General); 10th–90th percentile annual. Median $48,620.
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Loading live ordnance on a fighter is a real skill with almost no direct civilian equivalent, and that is the honest problem with this MOS. There is no civilian licence for 'aircraft ordnance'. Marine Corps COOL lists exactly one credential as directly related — NCATT Foreign Object Elimination — which is a minor certificate, not a career. The explosives SOC the Marine Corps maps you to (47-5032) covers mining and demolition blasters, which is a different trade with its own state and ATF licensing you will not have. Realistic civilian paths are defense-contractor munitions and weapons-loading work on government contracts (where the clearance and the ordnance experience are the product), or general maintenance. If you want this MOS to pay off outside a defense contract, use tuition assistance while you serve and leave with a credential in something else.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Same structural problem as 6531: the Marine Corps maps you to SOC 47-5032 (Explosives Workers, Ordnance Handling Experts and Blasters), but civilian blasting is a state-licensed and ATF-regulated trade and none of that licensing comes with the MOS. Marine Corps COOL lists the Certified Maintenance and Reliability Technician (CMRT) as the one directly-related credential — a maintenance cert, not an explosives one. The parts of this job that genuinely sell outside the military are hazmat handling, inventory/accountability of dangerous goods and material-handling-equipment licensing: COOL maps Certified Dangerous Goods Professional and Certified Hazardous Materials Manager here, and those DO have real civilian demand in logistics and shipping. Chase those two, not the blasting SOC.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is the strongest civilian crosswalk in the whole batch and the gap is the smallest — but it is not zero. Marine Corps COOL lists 24 directly-related credentials for 6694, including CompTIA A+, Network+, Security+, CySA+, Linux+ and Cisco CCNA/CCNP. None of them is awarded by the MOS school; each is an exam you sit. The difference between a Marine who separates from 6694 with Security+ and CCNA and one who separates with neither is roughly the difference between walking into a $70–90k network job and starting over at a help desk. DoD work also effectively requires Security+ under the DoD 8140/8570 baseline. COOL will fund these while you serve — this is the single highest-return use of COOL funding in this batch. Wage range shown is SOC 15-1244 (Network and Computer Systems Administrators).

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Read the salary figure carefully. NAVMC 1200.1M maps 6842 to SOC 19-2021 (Atmospheric and Space Scientists) — but the BLS entry requirement for that occupation is a bachelor's degree in meteorology or atmospheric science, and the National Weather Service will not hire you as a meteorologist without one. The MOS teaches you to forecast; it does not give you the degree that the civilian occupation is restricted on, so the wage range here is what degreed meteorologists earn, not what a separating 6842 walks into. The realistic path is: use tuition assistance and the GI Bill to finish an atmospheric-science degree while or after you serve — your operational forecasting experience then makes you a strong candidate rather than an unqualified one. Marine Corps COOL maps 6842 to GIS and remote-sensing technologist certifications, which are a genuine and much shorter alternative route. Note this MOS requires TS/SCI eligibility, which is itself a valuable asset.

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS national, May 2024, SOC 33-2011 (Firefighters); 10th–90th percentile annual. Median $59,530.
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: THE EMT GAP. This is the one to understand. Marine ARFF trains you to fight aircraft fires, and the DoD fire academy certifications you earn are genuinely portable — but Marine Corps COOL lists Emergency Medical Technician and Emergency Medical Responder as credentials still to be earned, not held. That matters because the overwhelming majority of civilian municipal fire departments require EMT certification as a condition of hire (many now require Paramedic), and most run their own civil-service exam, CPAT physical test and academy on top of it. A Marine ARFF veteran is a strong candidate — but is not a civilian firefighter on separation and cannot be hired as one without the EMT card. Close it while you are still in: get EMT through COOL funding, and get your DoD/Pro Board and IFSAC certifications documented before you check out. Federal (DoD civilian) fire departments are the fastest path because they recognise the same certifications you already hold.

It is an infantry-adjacent, field-heavy job: you live outdoors, carry weight and provide security as much as you shoot missiles.

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS national, May 2024, SOC 53-2022 (Airfield Operations Specialists); 10th–90th percentile annual. Median $56,750.
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Do not confuse this with being an air traffic controller. The MOS Manual maps 7240 to SOC 55-3015 — a military-only code with no BLS wage data — and the job is tactical air control, not civil air traffic control. Marine Corps COOL does map the FAA Control Tower Operator (CTO) certificate to this MOS as directly related, and if you can earn the CTO it is worth real money; but the FAA controller career itself has a hard requirement that catches people by surprise: entry-level FAA applicants must be under the age of 31, and the experienced-controller hiring path requires 52 consecutive weeks of actively separating air traffic plus being age 35 or under. A 7240 does not automatically accumulate that experience the way a 7257 does. The wage range shown here is the conservative one — SOC 53-2022, Airfield Operations Specialists — because quoting air-traffic-controller wages for this MOS would overstate it.

civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS national, May 2024, SOC 53-2021 (Air Traffic Controllers); 10th–90th percentile annual. Median $144,580.
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is the best civilian payoff in the batch, and it has a clock on it. You genuinely earn the FAA Control Tower Operator (CTO) certificate — NAVMC 1200.1M makes an FAA credential a formal requirement of the MOS — and Marine Corps COOL lists CTO and Aircraft Dispatcher as directly related. But holding the CTO is not the same as being hired by the FAA, and the FAA's own rules are unforgiving about age: entry-level applicants must be under the age of 31 (faa.gov). The route that matters for you is the FAA's 'experienced controller' announcement, which requires a minimum of 52 consecutive weeks of air traffic control experience involving the full-time active separation of air traffic after receipt of a facility rating, within five years of application, at a military or DoD facility — and you must be age 35 or under on the closing date. Translation: enlist young, get rated fast, make sure you bank 52 consecutive weeks of actual control time, keep the documentation, and apply before the window shuts. Marines who drift into non-controlling billets and let five years pass lose the qualification. Also note civilian ATC still requires passing the FAA medical and, for the entry path, the FAA Academy in Oklahoma City.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: A 7291 is the most employable air traffic controller the Marine Corps produces — but the FAA's age rules still bite, and they bite hardest here. The FAA's experienced-controller path requires you to be age 35 or under on the closing date of the application period, and a Marine typically does not reach GySgt/7291 until well into their thirties. Read that carefully: it is entirely possible to become one of the most qualified controllers in the Marine Corps and age out of FAA hiring. The 52 consecutive weeks of active traffic separation must also be within five years of application, and senior chiefs often stop controlling. If FAA employment is your goal, apply while you are a 7257/7252 — not after you make 7291. Private-sector and DoD-contract tower jobs (federal contract towers) have no such age limit and are the realistic route for senior controllers.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: NAVMC 1200.1M maps 7313 to SOC 55-3011 (Air Crew Members) — a military-only occupation code for which BLS publishes no civilian wage data, and Marine Corps COOL publishes no page for this MOS. So there is no defensible civilian salary figure and none is given here. The transferable asset is helicopter maintenance experience, and the credential that converts it is the FAA Airframe & Powerplant (A&P) certificate — which this MOS does NOT award. FAA rules let documented military maintenance experience count toward A&P eligibility, but you must still be signed off and pass the FAA written, oral and practical tests yourself. Civil helicopter operators (EMS, offshore, utility, tour) hire A&P mechanics; they do not hire 'former 7313s'. Start the A&P paperwork before you separate.

specialties.name: Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) Air Vehicle Operator
specialties.what_it_is: NOTE: NAVMC 1200.1M now titles MOS 7314 'Sensor Operator (SO)' — the older 'UAV Air Vehicle Operator' title is out of date. You fly the sensors on the MQ-9A Reaper: operating the cameras, targeting pods and other payloads from a ground control station, finding and tracking what the aircraft is looking at, and working as half of a crew with the aircraft commander. The decides on this MOS are among the hardest in the Marine Corps — a five-year enlistment, GT 110 with no waivers, no dual citizenship, a flight physical and TS/SCI.

entry_requirements.afqt_note: Minimum AFQT 31 to enlist in the Marine Corps; 50+ if you hold a GED or nontraditional diploma (marines.com enlistment requirements). This is separate from the MOS line-score requirement above.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Top Secret / SCI (single-scope background investigation / T5); must be initiated before the BUQ course
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Marine Corps enlistment standard).
entry_requirements.physical: U.S. citizen and NOT a dual citizen (no waivers). Must pass the normal colour perception test (no waivers). Must pass a Class IV flight physical before the Basic UAS Qualification course.
entry_requirements.other: Requires a five-year term of enlistment. GT 110 minimum with no waivers. Awarded the Marine Unmanned Aircraft System breast insignia on receipt of the MOS.

civilian_titles: Electro-Mechanical and Mechatronics Technologists and Technicians
Airline Pilots, Copilots, and Flight Engineers
civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS national, May 2024, SOC 17-3024 (Electro-Mechanical and Mechatronics Technologists and Technicians); 10th–90th percentile annual. Median $70,760.
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Two gaps, and the second one is the one nobody mentions. First: the only directly-related civilian credential Marine Corps COOL maps to 7314 is the FAA Part 107 Remote Pilot certificate — a roughly $175 knowledge test that a civilian teenager can pass in a weekend. Flying an MQ-9 for the Marine Corps does not make you an FAA-certificated pilot of anything, and the manual's SOC list even includes 53-2011 (Airline Pilots) which is flatly misleading — no pilot certificate is conferred. Second: the civilian large-drone industry is small; the jobs that actually pay for MQ-9 experience are defence-contractor billets, and what makes you hireable for those is the TS/SCI clearance and the documented flight hours far more than the flying skill. So: keep the clearance current, log and document every hour, get Part 107 (cheap, do it immediately), and understand that the wage range shown — SOC 17-3024, Electro-Mechanical and Mechatronics Technicians — is the realistic technical analog, not airline pay.

specialties.name: Small Unmanned Aerial System (SUAS) Operator
specialties.what_it_is: NOTE: NAVMC 1200.1M titles MOS 7316 'Small Unmanned Aircraft System (SUAS) Specialist'. You operate the small drones that a ground unit carries with it — launching, flying and recovering them, and running the sensors to give the unit its own overhead picture. Compared with 7314 this is a smaller aircraft, a shorter pipeline and a far more field-and-infantry-adjacent job, but it still requires a five-year enlistment and GT 110.

entry_requirements.afqt_note: Minimum AFQT 31 to enlist in the Marine Corps; 50+ if you hold a GED or nontraditional diploma (marines.com enlistment requirements). This is separate from the MOS line-score requirement above.
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Marine Corps enlistment standard).
entry_requirements.physical: U.S. citizenship. Normal colour vision and depth perception (waiver requires an aeromedical officer). Must meet the annual UAS medical screening exam (Manual of the Medical Department, Article 15-63).
entry_requirements.other: Requires a five-year term of enlistment. GT 110 minimum (waivers reviewed by the 7316 occupational field specialists). Awarded the Marine Unmanned Aircraft System breast insignia.

civilian_titles: Electro-Mechanical and Mechatronics Technologists and Technicians
civilian_crosswalk.salary_source: BLS OEWS national, May 2024, SOC 17-3024 (Electro-Mechanical and Mechatronics Technologists and Technicians); 10th–90th percentile annual. Median $70,760.
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: The honest headline: the civilian credential this MOS maps to is the FAA Part 107 Remote Pilot certificate, and Part 107 is a knowledge test that costs about $175 and requires no military service whatsoever. That is the only credential Marine Corps COOL lists as directly related to 7316. Military SUAS experience makes you better at flying drones than a fresh Part 107 holder, but it does not give you a credential a civilian employer can distinguish from theirs — you have to go get the same card they have. Do it while you are in, through COOL. Where the experience genuinely pays is in survey, inspection, mapping and public-safety drone work combined with a real technical skill (GIS, surveying, thermography) or a clearance-holding contractor job. The wage range shown is SOC 17-3024 (Electro-Mechanical and Mechatronics Technicians); COOL also maps remote-sensing technician roles here.

specialties.name: Tactical Systems Operator/Mission Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: Tactical Systems Operators / Mission Specialists are enlisted aircrew on Marine transport and refuelling aircraft. You perform tactical navigation, threat assessment and mission planning, and work in-flight tasks including aerial refuelling, aerial delivery, low-level and night-vision flight. You fly as a crew member — you are not the pilot.

entry_requirements.afqt_note: Minimum AFQT 31 to enlist in the Marine Corps; 50+ if you hold a GED or nontraditional diploma (marines.com enlistment requirements). This is separate from the MOS line-score requirement above.

entry_requirements.other: The MOS Manual lists no ASVAB line score for 7372 and states that VMGR-452 assigns this MOS — it is an assigned/lateral aircrew MOS reached from inside the squadron, not one you enlist directly into. Grade range MGySgt to LCpl.

civilian_titles: Airline Pilots, Copilots, and Flight Engineers
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Read this one carefully, because the official crosswalk is actively misleading. NAVMC 1200.1M maps MOS 7372 to SOC 53-2011 — 'Airline Pilots, Copilots, and Flight Engineers' — an occupation whose BLS median exceeds $200,000. This MOS confers no FAA pilot certificate of any kind. Publishing airline-pilot wages against an enlisted aircrew MOS would badly mislead you, so no salary figure is given here rather than a wrong one. What the job actually gives you is aircrew experience, SERE qualification and a security clearance. If you want to fly for a living, the path runs through civilian flight training and FAA certificates (Private → Instrument → Commercial → ATP, roughly 1,500 hours for ATP) paid for largely by you and the GI Bill — your military aircrew time does not count toward those hours unless you were the pilot. Marine Corps COOL publishes no page for 7372.

specialties.name: Congressional Marine Liaison
specialties.what_it_is: An enlisted Marine serving as the Marine Corps' liaison to the U.S. Congress, working out of the Office of Legislative Affairs. You support the relationship between the Service and Capitol Hill — briefings, congressional inquiries, escorting, and staff work. It is a Washington staff job, not a field job.

entry_requirements.afqt_note: Minimum AFQT 31 to enlist in the Marine Corps; 50+ if you hold a GED or nontraditional diploma (marines.com enlistment requirements). This is separate from the MOS line-score requirement above.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Secret, with eligibility for Top Secret
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Marine Corps enlistment standard).
entry_requirements.other: NOT AN ENTRY-LEVEL MOS. This is a 'Free MOS'. NAVMC 1200.1M requires you to complete a 12-month tour as a Congressional Fellow on Capitol Hill and then serve a minimum of 18 months within the Office of Legislative Affairs. The certifying authority is the Legislative Assistant to the Commandant. You reach this MOS as an established senior Marine, not from the recruiting office.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: NAVMC 1200.1M lists 'Related Standard Occupational Classification: None' for 8018 — the Marine Corps claims no civilian occupation for it — and Marine Corps COOL publishes no credential map. No BLS wage figure can honestly be attached, so none is given. In practice the experience is genuinely valuable in government relations, lobbying and congressional staff work, but that market hires on network and demonstrated Hill experience rather than on any certificate, and typically expects a degree. No credential closes this gap because no credential exists for it.
